opts: Commit to the backhand since you read it first — drop a butterfly over there and seal it
Stop your feet immediately and try to reset — don't fully commit to the fake
Dive across flat on your pads to try to cover both posts after they pull back across
Skate out to the top of the crease and challenge to cut the shooting angle down a lot
Stay tight on the post and trust it — the post takes away the short side, so don't move off it even if they cut across
Push off the post and move with them — they've changed the angle and you need to stay square to the puck
Drop into a butterfly on the post and seal the ice along the pad to take away any low shot as they cut across the crease
Come out into the crease to challenge the cut — step out to cut the angle as they cross in front of your net to try to score
Say nothing and trust your D to sort the front of the net out for the open attacker themselves
Call out loudly to your nearest forward — 'Front! Front!' — so they know to pick up the open player
Skate out of your crease to cover the open attacker in front of the net yourself
Drop into your butterfly early and wait for the pass to come out of the corner
Stay in your current low position — you're already set, so trust it and let the point shooter try from distance
Move out to the top of your crease immediately — the point shot requires a different position than the low play
Drop into butterfly right away — a point shot usually goes low through traffic, so get your pads down to take the bottom
Back up to your goal line and square up — giving yourself more time to see the shot is the best play on a point shot
Give up on the puck and just reset — once you're down you can't recover in time, so accept it and get ready for the next shift
Throw your stick or extend whatever body part you can reach into the path of the puck — any contact can change its direction
Wave your glove at the ref and call for a penalty since you were knocked down — ask for a delayed call on the shot right now
Stay still and trust the post to save you — the puck usually hits the pipe from that angle, so freezing gives you a lucky chance
Stay on the right post and trust your teammates — your D should take the backdoor, so hold and cover the half-wall
T-push hard across to the left post — the backdoor player is the immediate danger, the half-wall shooter is not
Come out of your crease toward the puck carrier on the half-wall — challenge the shooter and take away the shooting angle now
Drop into a butterfly on the right post — seal the ice short side and let your defenseman take the backdoor player cutting
Nothing — random body language and there's no real pattern you can read from a dropped elbow
They're likely shooting high — a dropping elbow often indicates a high release
They're going to pass instead — a dropping elbow usually means they're about to sauce a pass to a teammate
They'll deke first before shooting — dropping the elbow is how shooters set up a deke to the forehand to score
Spread your legs wide apart in your stance — a wide base covers the low corners of the net and keeps you balanced on the ice
Keep your pads together or slightly overlapping and squeeze your knees — make the 5-hole as small as possible
Drop into a butterfly immediately on the ice — getting your pads flat seals the 5-hole and takes any low shot between your legs
Stand with feet at shoulder width for balance — a normal stance gives you the mobility to react to any shot, 5-hole included
Freeze the puck right away to kill the clock — any stoppage you can get late in a one-goal game burns time off the clock
Leave it for your team — freezing it gives the other team a faceoff in your zone with 90 seconds left, which is dangerous
Kick the puck away from the front of your net — send it anywhere away from the danger area and let your team sort out the puck
Throw the puck to the corner with your glove — get it out of the slot area and let your D retrieve it behind the goal line safe
Say nothing and trust your defensemen to see the forwards sneaking in behind them on the play themselves
Call out immediately — 'Two coming!' — to warn your D that forwards are breaking behind them
Wait a few seconds to see if your D notice the sneaking forwards themselves before you do anything from the net
Yell for a line change so a fresh group can come on and sort the coverage out
Stay in the middle of your crease and wait for the puck to appear on the other side
Move with them — push to the right post as they come around and seal it. Don't let them get the post.
Come out past the crease to try and stop them behind the net before they wrap
Drop into the butterfly right away and slide across behind you
Skate all the way out to the top of the circles to cut their shooting angle down to almost nothing
Come to the top of the crease, stay on your feet as long as possible, and force them to make the first move
Drop to the butterfly as soon as they cross the blue line to seal low ice
Stay planted right on your goal line and wait for them to shoot
They should have stayed on the bench longer — too many players out causes confusion, so a quicker line change fixes it
They left their man open — in the D-zone, everyone needs to have a player covered before chasing the puck
They should have gone straight to the net front — the slot is the most dangerous area, so that's where they need to be
They skated too slow on that play — if they had chased harder they would have won the puck battle and the possession
You were skating too slow to keep up with the play — if you had more speed through neutral you'd have caught the puck
You entered the zone before the puck offside. You need to time your entry so the puck crosses the line first.
You should have stopped at center ice and waited — a trailing forward stays back at the red line until the puck is in
The puck carrier was too slow getting over the line — if they had pushed the pace harder you wouldn't have gotten ahead
The forwards weren't skating hard enough — if all three had chased with more speed they'd have caught the puck first
All three forwards went deep — at least one forward needs to stay high to prevent the breakout pass through the middle
The defensemen were too far back at the blue line — they should have stepped up to pinch and kept the puck in the zone
The goalie didn't stop the breakout pass across the ice — goalies should play the puck when the team forechecks hard
You should have pinched harder and finished the check — if you commit you have to follow through, and a half pinch got beat
You pinched when the puck wasn't clearly yours to win — if you can't get there first, hold the line instead of gambling
Your partner should have covered for you — when one D pinches, the other D rotates to the middle, and your partner didn't
The forward got lucky on the bounce — the puck took a weird bounce off the boards that nobody could have predicted
The defensemen were out of position on the turnover — they were too deep in the offensive zone and couldn't get back in time
The forwards didn't transition immediately — when you lose the puck, you turn and skate back right away, no watching
The goalie came out too far from the net — he was too aggressive playing the puck and couldn't recover in time on the rush
The coach called the wrong play on that shift — the system your team ran left too many forwards deep in the offensive zone
The ref made a bad call on the change — at this age refs often blow line changes dead that were actually legal on both sides of the ice, and this one clearly shouldn't have been called down
Too many men on the ice — the fresh player jumped on before the tired player was close enough to the bench. The outgoing player needs to be within arm's reach of the bench.
Offside at center — the fresh player jumped onto the ice past the red line while the puck was still in the defensive zone, so the play was ruled offside by the ref
Icing on the tired player — the tired player skated past center without playing the puck so the ref blew it down for icing against the team making the change
Turned faster on the turn — if you had whipped your body around quicker the defender wouldn't have caught up to you at all
Turned away from the pressure — to your left — protecting the puck with your body between you and the defender
Shot the puck immediately off the boards — a quick wrist shot toward the net was better than trying to turn into the defender
Called for help louder from your teammates — a quick yell brings a winger low to support you along the boards before the hit
Your team backs off and resets — let the other team complete the pass and set up your D structure at your own blue line
Your second forward, who stayed high, intercepts the pass in the slot exactly why they stayed high
Everyone collapses low to the corner — all three forwards go down to pin the puck against the boards and win it back
The play resets at center ice — the blind pass usually gets knocked into neutral and both teams regroup for a new cycle
You keep skating backward to the outside and let them cut — stay in your lane since forcing outside is still the goal
You pivot and match their cut — stay on their inside hip and use your body to close them off
You stop and watch the cut — let them cross the middle so you can see where they're going before you react
You go for a big hip check — when they cut back inside they expose themselves, so step up and knock them off the puck
They dump the puck in and change — dumping it deep is the safe play after a turnover so tired players can change lines
They attack with speed — the other team is caught deep, so this is a rush chance. Go now before they recover.
They pass the puck back to the goalie to reset — starting a controlled breakout is better than rushing in with no support
They wait for everyone to get set in position — regrouping at center ice gives your team a full five-player attack in
The open player passes the puck back across — they send it to the left half-wall to reset since they don't have a clean shot
The open player has a one-timer or clean shot — the PK can't recover across the ice fast enough, so this is a prime scoring chance
The open player skates it to the corner — they carry it down low behind the goal line to set up a cycle with the forwards
The play dies — the PK recovers too fast so the open player just holds the puck and waits for everyone to set up again
Play continues normally — puck came from behind the red line but nobody touched it, so the ref lets it go
The ref blows the whistle for icing — faceoff comes back to your zone and your tired players can't change
Your team gets a power play on the shot — firing the puck the length of the ice under pressure is legal, so a clean clear
The other team gets a minor — a D panicking and shooting the puck down is delay of game, so refs call two minutes on it
You hold the puck forever on your skate — keeping it pinned kills time and tires the defender, and the ref will eventually whistle a stoppage for you
You kick the puck to your stick and make a quick pass to your linemate before the next defender arrives — you won the battle, now use it
You leave the puck and skate away — once you've tied up the defender your job is done, so release to the slot and let a teammate grab it
You shoot the puck on net right off your skate — any puck toward the net from the boards can cause a rebound that a teammate can tap in
You dump the puck deep and chase — a flat-footed D won't react fast, but dumping it in is still safer than carrying across the line
You use your speed to carry the puck wide around the flat-footed defender — your speed advantage makes the carry the best option
You stop at the blue line and wait — let the defender commit first, then make your read once your wingers catch up for the entry
You pass it backward to the trailer — drop the puck to your center at center ice and let them attack with fresh speed through neutral
